# Transcrição: 09- 51 Lost  Found

- **Fonte:** 09- 51 Lost  Found
- **Duração:** 11:35
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:02]

All right, here's another way that we're going to pull stories from the past into the present. Recently, I sat down and I made a list of all the things that I had lost in my life that I sort of was regretful about losing. We lose a lot of things in our lives. We either misplace them or we don't keep track of something that we should have. We don't recognize the importance of something at the time and then we wish we still had it. What I discovered in making my list of all the things I had ever lost is every single one of them is a story. Most of them are sort of tragic and unfortunate stories, but sadly those make really good stories. I had a list of about 23 things over the course of my life that I sort of wish I still had, objects that were either misplaced or thrown away foolishly or frankly, I don't know where they are right now and I may never find them again. A few examples that I found. When I was 12 years old, I went to camp for the first time overnight camp and I was pretty nervous. I was the youngest person in my Boy Scout troop and I sleepwalk all the time even to this day. So I was worried about sleepwalking into the woods, which I often did. I'd wake up in the middle of the forest. I'd be alone. I'd have to sit at a tree until the sun came up. It was a nerve-wracking time. And so one of the ways I sort of dealt with the nights was I brought a stuffed animal with me. A little orange stuffed animal, a dog named Roscoe, and I would put him in my sleeping bag because I couldn't let any of my friends know that I brought a stuffed animal with me. So he was all the way on the bottom of my sleeping bag. And it was amazing how when I climbed into it at night, just feeling Roscoe up against my feet, this inanimate object that I knew objectively was just stuffing and cloth. Somehow because it had an eyes and a nose and a mouth and a name, it brought me great comfort when I was a kid. And then one night I went sleepwalking, I think with Roscoe, and I lost him somewhere in the woods of Rhode Island. And I went looking for him in the morning and I could not find him and he was lost forever. And that was 40 years ago. And I still think of Roscoe. I think I think of Roscoe because of the comfort he brought me and how I sort of felt as a kid like I betrayed him. I took him into the woods and left him behind and never recovered him. And it still bothers me. Can you feel how that's a story? When I was 19 years old, I met Cammy Norris in Laconia, New Hampshire, and we started sort of casually dating. I lived in Massachusetts about 100 miles away. So when we were not seeing each other, we would write letters to each other. And Cammy wrote the best letters. She was six years older than me. I was a

## [03:02 - 06:08]

kid who didn't really have parents or adults in his life. And so when Cammy wrote me letters, letters from this sort of independent woman living on her own, that contained the wisdom and the insight that I was sorely lacking as a kid without any parents or adult supervision of any kind. She was romantically attached to me and we were dating and, you know, we were really into each other. But she was also just a really smart and wise person. And I loved every one of her letters. I kept them in a box. And even when we stopped dating, I would go back and I would reread her letters. And every time I read one of her letters, I'd find something like a nugget of wisdom that I had not seen before. And then eventually sort of my life became messy. I became homeless and I lost a lot of the things that were precious to me, including that box of letters. I hate the fact that I don't have those letters today. Can you feel how that's a story? When I was 16 years old, I flew to Pasadena, California to march in the Rose Bowl. And on another plane, flying also to Pasadena was my girlfriend, Laura. And because we were going to be apart for six hours in the air, we recorded cassette tapes for each other. My cassette tapes contained music. I made a mixtape of all the songs that talked about love to convince Laura that I loved her. But when I pressed play on Laura's cassette, it was her speaking to me, telling me stories about her family, reading shells, silverstein poetry to me, singing yesterday while the player piano in her living room pounded away. It was beautiful. It was the girl I loved speaking directly to me. When I became homeless, I lost those tapes as well. And I miss them. But I really miss them today because Laura died when she was about 42 of cancer. And she had two little girls, two little girls whose lives are absent of a mother. And I happen to have photos and letters that Laura wrote to me. I have lots of things to pass on to those two little girls when they're old enough to hear those stories, the stories that Laura asked me to tell them before she died. But I wish I had those tapes. I think it would be an amazing thing for those two girls to hear their mother at the age of 16, singing a song and reciting poetry and telling me stories. I can't stand the fact that I've lost those tapes. Can you feel how that's a story? I wish I had those cassette tapes for me. But I really wish I had them today for Laura's girls. Laura passed away when she was 42 of cancer, far too early. And her girls

## [06:08 - 09:11]

were young at the time. Before she died, she asked me to tell the girls the story of her teenage years when they were old enough to hear the stories. And so over time, I've been collecting photos and collecting stories and gathering the letters that Laura used to write to me. I have a box full of stuff for those girls. I really wish those cassettes were in that box. I think it would be extraordinary to pass cassettes like that over to girls who no longer have a mom so that they could hear her at the age of 16, singing the Beatles and reciting poetry and telling her boyfriend at the time the stories of her family. I hate the fact that I lost those tapes when I became homeless. They're the things I wish I had the most in this world that I have lost. Can you feel how that's a story? About 18 years ago, Alicia Dix, my wife, who at the time was Alicia Green, told me that she liked me. We were sitting in a car together at the end of an evening having fun, and she admitted to liking me. And I said to her, I'm flattered and nothing else. And so she got out of the car and she drove home. And it wasn't until I was halfway home that I realized, oh my God, I said I'm flattered. I had been in love with Alicia Green for a year. And at the moment she finally told me that she liked me, the best I could do is I'm flattered. So I called her, but as always, she wasn't picking up her phone because she never picks up her phone. And so the next morning when I came to school, because we were teaching together, I found a note on my desk from Alicia, apologizing for admitting that she liked me, apologizing for creating an awkward moment between us, and promising me that we could just be friends and that all of that romance could go away. I took that note and I charged up to her classroom. And I threw it at her and I said, no, I'm crazy. I like you too. I'm crazy about you. And we we had a moment. And I don't know where that note went. I remember tossing it on her desk. I remember rejecting it. And it was lost to time. We didn't hold on to it. Wouldn't it be amazing if I had that note today to show the kids? I wish I had it. It's a story. Can you see how it's a story? It was a story that I had completely forgotten about a moment between my wife and I, a critical moment in our relationship that I had forgotten until I sat down and said to myself, what are some of the things in my past objects that I wish I

## [09:11 - 11:35]

still had? What are those lost things, those lost items? Every time I found one, it was attached to a story. And so that's what I want you to do. Sit down, clear the table, give yourself half an hour, give yourself an hour, give yourself a day, sit down and make a list. Maybe spend the next week thinking about this question and adding to the list, all of the things that you've lost over time, you misplaced, you threw away, you lost track of, what are those items? Every one of them, I promise, there'll be a story attached. You'll find new stories. Most of them are going to break your heart. But that's okay. Sometimes the best stories break our hearts. And when we recover the past, when we when we bring the past forward, even when we're bringing forward those heartbreaking stories, we feel better about our lives. We feel fuller and more complete. We have really great things to tell other people and to tell ourselves. Go find those lost things. Hiding in there, you'll find stories. Most of them are going to break your heart. But that's okay. Not every story is a happy one. But they're all important. As much as I miss Roscoe, I'm glad I have the story. As much as I wish I had Cammie's letters, I'm glad that I am remembering the things and the letters and I'm able to share the story with you. I really wish I had Laura's cassette tapes. But I'm glad I have the story about them and the memory. And boy, do I wish I had that letter that Alicia wrote to me 18 years ago. It would be a great thing to show our kids. But it's a great story to tell them. Not every story has to be happy. But every story is important. Even the ones about the things in our lives that we wish we still had. So take time. Think about those lost things in your life. Make a list. And that list will be filled with stories.
